text:
There is a moment before rain fully commits to itself — a hesitation in the air, a held breath that the atmosphere releases incrementally, as though the sky were reconsidering its decision even as it acts upon it. The first drops arrive not as rain but as punctuation, isolated and deliberate, each one striking pavement or leaf or the back of an upturned hand with the quality of an announcement rather than an event. Something shifts in the light, a bruising of the afternoon that is not quite darkness but is the memory of darkness, the air suddenly wearing its weight differently, the way a room changes when someone who has just wept walks into it.

What arrests me about this particular threshold is how it reorganizes attention. The skin becomes suddenly philosophical, each nerve ending asking questions about boundary and permeability. You notice the smell before you can name it — petrichor, that ancient word for something far older than language, the scent of earth releasing what it has held in dry custody for days or weeks, an exhale of stored memory. There is grief in it, and also relief, the way grief and relief are so often indistinguishable at the moment of their arrival.

The drops quicken. What was sporadic becomes a pattern, what was question becomes declaration. And yet even now, even as rain becomes undeniably rain, that first feeling persists underneath — the feeling of standing at the very edge of a change, uncertain whether to run for shelter or remain, face lifted, in that rare human posture of willingness to be altered by whatever is about to arrive.
